rulings.Case Title: MILAN KANGRGA VS. RETIREMENT BOARD OF THE SAN FRANCISCO EMPLOYEES
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus Pursuant To California Civil Procedure Code 1094.5
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2015, Line 2, PETITIONER MILAN KANGRGA'S Motion For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus Pursuant To California Civil Procedure Code 1094.5. Denied. The evidence supports the administrative decision. The Administrative Law Judge primarily relied on the findings of Dr. Allem in reaching his decision. The evidence establishes that Dr. Allem performed a comprehensive physical examination and thoroughly reviewing Petitioner's medical records. Dr. Allem reached the conclusion that "[Petitioner's] work as a police officer did not cause or contribute to his coronary artery disease on any biological basis. His CAD, its evolution and extent and need for treatment has been determined by the confluence of his multiple personal risk factors - age, sex, family history, smoking hypertension, obesity, hyperlipidemia, and sleep apnea. His risk of coronary artery disease was determined exclusively on these well recognized biological risk factors, and was not increased by the fact that he was a police officer." (AR 66.) Additionally, the Administrative Law Judge relied on the fact that Petitioner "was not compliant with the medications and lifestyle changes recommended to him by his physicians." (AR 14.) This fact is evidenced by Petitioner's medical records. (AR 93-95, 108-109, 116-122, 125-126, 166-170.) Thus, the evidence supports the Administrative Law Judge's conclusion that Respondent presented sufficient evidence to overcome the presumption provided for in San Francisco Administrative Code sec. 16.85. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY DEWITT VS. CRAZY PROTOCOL COMMUNICATIONS, INC. A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Serfice Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2015, Line 3, DEFENDANT EHARMONY, INC.'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons. Granted. A motion to quash is the proper procedural mechanism for challenging a Doe amendment. (See Optical Surplus Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 776.) The evidence submitted by Defendant establishes that Plaintiff was not truly ignorant of Defendant's identity at the time the Doe amendment was filed, as required by CCP sec. 474. The evidence shows that Plaintiff was aware that he had received alleged SPAM emails from Defendant in as early as March 2013. (Katz Decl., Exs. B and C.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: KLATT, SUZANNE, AS SURVIVING HEIR OF PETER VS. RIGLER, JEFFERY, AS PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Jeffrey Rigler'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Guardian Ad Litem To Answer Deposition Questions And To Produce Requested Documents; Related Request For Sanctions [Ccp2025.480(A)-(K)].
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday April 21, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT JEFFREY RIGLER'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Guardian Ad Litem To Answer Deposition Questions And To Produce Requested Documents; Related Request For Sanctions [Ccp2025.480(A)-(K)]. (part 2 of 2). The obligation to meet and confer requires counsel to "attempt to talk the matter over, compare their views, consult, and deliberate." Townsend v. Superior Court, 61 Cal. App. 4th 1431, 1439 (1998). Defendant's meet and confer efforts did not meet this standard. Defendant's March 6 letter and March 24 email did not constitute the kind of substantive exchange envisioned by Townsend. Indeed, the March 6 letter did not even raise all of the document requests and deposition questions that are the subject of Defendant's motion. Accordingly, the motion and sanctions request must be denied. In the event that Defendant contests the tentative ruling, the Judge Pro Tem requests that Defendant be prepared to also address why no separate statement was provided with respect to the deposition questions pursuant to California Rule of Court 3.1345(a). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tentative ruling) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KLATT, SUZANNE, AS SURVIVING HEIR OF PETER VS. RIGLER, JEFFERY, AS PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Plaintiff;S Guardian Ad Litem To Answer Deposition Questions And To Produce Requested Documents; Related Request For Sanctions [Ccp2025.480(A)-(K)]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday April 21, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT JEFFREY RIGLER'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Guardian Ad Litem To Answer Deposition Questions And To Produce Requested Documents; Related Request For Sanctions [Ccp2025.480(A)-(K)]. (part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Anthony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Judge Pro Tem finds that Defendant failed to adequately meet and confer prior to filing the instant motion. See CCP §2025.480(b). Defendant's meet and confer efforts consisted of one letter dated March 6 that listed document requests and deposition questions as to which responses were sought. It provided no discussion of the reasons why responses were sought or why Defendant believed the guardian at litem's ("GAL") objections were not well founded. Several days later, GAL's counsel advised Defendant that GAL had been hospitalized for bronchitis but affirmed that further responses to the discovery would be forthcoming when GAL recovered. Defendant made no further effort to meet and confer; rather, on March 24, Defense counsel sent an email stating that because no further responses had been served, a motion to compel would be filed. The email did not inquire about the GAL's illness or ask when the promised responses would be provided. Defendant filed this motion the following day. In the motion, Defendant seeks to compel responses to 20 document requests and four deposition questions. However, only 14 of the 20 document requests and two of the four deposition questions were raised in the March 6 meet and confer letter. (continued to part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LAI SIM LAI VS. DAVID EUGENE REISER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Of Opposition To Defendant David Eugene Reisers Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2015, Line 6, REAL PARTY IN INTEREST CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion Of Opposition To Defendant David Eugene Reiser's Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement. The motion contesting the settlement is granted. The record does not disclose substantial competent evidence showing that the settlement is in the ballpark within the meaning of Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assoc., (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488. See Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1350, n. 6. While defendant is paying his insurance policy limit, there is no evidence regarding his financial status or the other Tech-Bilt factors. There is insufficient evidence regarding a rough approximation of plaintiff's total recovery. Defendant argues that only a small amount of the medical bills have been paid, but cites no competent evidence to support that assertion. rulings.Case Title: ABERCROMBIE & FITCH STORES INC A CORPORATION VS. LARK CREEK CAFE, INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Prayer For Punitive Damages
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2015, Line 12, DEFENDANT LARK CREEK CAFE, INC.'S Motion To Strike Prayer For Punitive Damages. The motion to strike the claim for punitive damages is granted without leave to amend. Under Civil Code section 3294, malice is defined as either (1) conduct which is intended by the defendant to cause injury to the plaintiff or (2) "despicable conduct which is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others." (Italics added.) " 'Despicable conduct' is conduct which is so vile, base, contemptible, miserable, wretched or loathsome that it would be looked down upon and despised by ordinary decent people." Mock v. Michigan Millers Mutual Ins. Co. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 306, 331. Here, the complaint alleges conduct that sounds in negligence, not "despicable" conduct. Plaintiff's allegations that defendant acted intentionally are conclusory. The court finds McDonnell v. American Trust Co. (1955) 130 Cal.App.2d 296, 300, to be persuasive. That case did not allow punitive damages in similar circumstances and the decision analyzed a version of CC 3294 that allowed the recovery of punitive damages in a more liberal manner than the present version of the statute.
